advantages: Zero-centered outputs (tanh(0) = 0, odd symmetry) reduce systematic bias in downstream weight updates compared to always-positive sigmoid activations.
Output range (−1, 1) matches naturally with data normalized to [−1, 1] and provides symmetric positive/negative representation.
Maximum derivative 1 at x = 0 is four times sigmoid's peak (0.25), giving stronger gradients near the origin during early training.
Smooth, differentiable everywhere with a simple closed-form derivative 1 − tanh²(x) for efficient backpropagation.
Bounded activations prevent hidden units from exploding in magnitude — useful in RNN hidden state updates across time steps.
disadvantages: Saturates for large |x|, driving gradient 1 − tanh²(x) toward zero and causing vanishing gradients in deep saturated networks.
Computationally more expensive than ReLU (exponentials vs a max/threshold) and generally slower on modern accelerators for large layers.
Not ideal for binary probability outputs — sigmoid's (0, 1) range is the correct match for Bernoulli probabilities and [0, 1] gates.
Less common than ReLU in deep feedforward stacks today; using tanh in very deep MLPs often trains slower without careful initialization.
Sensitive to input scale — if pre-activations are too large, most units saturate early and learning stalls until weights rescale.
